schools.summary: The birth of Cain: 'and Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain, and said, I have gotten a man from the LORD' — the first human birth, the firstborn of mankind (the propagation of the race beginning, Eve, the mother of all living, 3:20, bearing her first son, Cain, whose name relates to 'gotten' or 'acquired'); Eve's exclamation 'I have gotten a man from the LORD' expressing her sense of God's gift and perhaps her hope in the promise (3:15, some reading it as a hope, mistaken, that Cain was the promised seed who would bruise the serpent, the words rendered by some 'I have gotten a man, the LORD,' or 'a man from the LORD,' whether or not she thought Cain the Messiah, she acknowledging the child as God's gift and laying hold of the promise); the believing recognition of God's hand in the gift of children (her words mingling gratitude with hope, in the birth of her son seeing God's faithfulness and the continuance of the race through which the promised seed would come) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Adam knew Eve his wife; and she conceived, and bare Cain, and said, I have gotten a man from the LORD
the birth of Cain and Eve's words
the first human birth, the child acknowledged as God's gift
Eve's hope perhaps laid on the promised seed

schools.summary: The birth of Abel and the callings: 'and she again bare his brother Abel. And Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground' — Eve bearing a second son, Abel (whose name means 'vapor' or 'breath,' perhaps foreshadowing his brief life), the two brothers Cain and Abel the first of the race born of woman; 'Abel was a keeper of sheep (a shepherd), but Cain was a tiller of the ground (a farmer)' (the brothers following different occupations, Abel a herdsman, Cain a cultivator, both honest callings, the two basic forms of early human labor — pastoral and agricultural — the division of labor in the first family); the brothers' different occupations both lawful and honorable (God providing for human life through such labors, the difference in calling not the ground of God's later regard, vv.4-5, which rested on faith and the heart not the occupation, both shepherd and farmer able to serve God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: she again bare his brother Abel. And Abel was a keeper of sheep, but Cain was a tiller of the ground
the birth of Abel and the brothers' callings
the two brothers in their honest, different callings
both lawful, the difference not the ground of God's regard

schools.summary: Cain's offering: 'and in process of time it came to pass, that Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering unto the LORD' — at some appointed or customary time ('in process of time,' at the end of days), the brothers bringing offerings to God (the bringing of offerings showing that the worship of God by sacrifice was already practiced, man approaching God with gifts); 'Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering' (Cain, the farmer, bringing of his produce, the fruit of the ground, the offering of the kind suited to his calling, whether the kind of offering or the heart behind it was at fault considered in vv.4-5, Cain's offering a bloodless gift of the ground's produce); 'an offering (minchah, a gift) unto the LORD' (the bringing of an offering showing that sacrifice and the worship of God were established, men acknowledging God and bringing him offerings, the practice of offering pointing to the recognition of God's claim and, in the right offering, of man's need, the question of acceptance, vv.4-5, turning on the offerer and the offering) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Clarke.

themes: in process of time it came to pass, that Cain brought of the fruit of the ground an offering unto the LORD
Cain bringing an offering of the fruit of the ground
the worship of God by sacrifice already practiced
Cain's bloodless gift of the ground's produce

schools.summary: Clarke (with Gill): Abel brought of the firstlings of his flock and the fat — an animal sacrifice, the firstborn lambs, frequently used in sacrifice and a fitting type of Christ, the slain Lamb, Abel's offering involving the death of an animal and the shedding of blood; the LORD had respect to Abel because, by faith, he offered 'a more excellent (pleiona, fuller) sacrifice' (Hebrews 11:4), professing faith in the promised Messiah, feeling his need of atonement, offering a vicarious, bloody sacrifice prefiguring Christ; the kind of offering (a blood-sacrifice owning sin and atonement) marking his faith, in contrast to Cain's bloodless fruit of the ground which acknowledged God as Creator but not the need of atonement.

Calvin: God 'had respect unto Abel' FIRST, and then to his offering — the offerer's faith and heart accepted before the gift, God looking first to the man and then to his sacrifice, the difference between the brothers lying in faith and the heart, not merely in the matter offered; Gill and JFB join faith and the fitting sacrifice (Abel's faith, expressed in the right, atoning blood-sacrifice, accepted, perhaps God showing his acceptance by fire from heaven consuming it); the person must be accepted before the offering, and Abel's faith made his sacrifice acceptable, while Cain, lacking faith, was rejected with his offering.

debates.question: Why was Abel's offering accepted and Cain's rejected?

sides.position: Abel's faith and heart — the person (the offerer) accepted before the gift; the difference lay in faith, not the matter (Hebrews 11:4)
The kind of offering — Abel's atoning blood-sacrifice (the firstling, prefiguring Christ) vs Cain's bloodless fruit, which owned no need of atonement
Both joined — Abel's faith expressed in the fitting, atoning sacrifice

themes: Abel... brought of the firstlings of his flock and of the fat thereof. And the LORD had respect unto Abel and to his offering
Abel's accepted offering
the crux of the ground of acceptance (faith and heart, or the atoning sacrifice)
the firstling a type of Christ, the slain Lamb

schools.summary: The rejection and Cain's wrath: 'but unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect. And Cain was very wroth, and his countenance fell' — God not accepting Cain or his offering (the rejection of the person and his gift, Cain, lacking faith, Hebrews 11:4, and bringing a bloodless offering without the sense of sin and atonement, not accepted, the contrast with Abel, v.4, pointed); 'Cain was very wroth (greatly angry)' (instead of repenting and amending, Cain filled with wrath, his anger directed against God, who rejected him, and against Abel, who was accepted, the sin of envy and rage rising in his heart, the rejection, which should have led to repentance, kindling wrath); 'his countenance fell' (Cain's face falling, betraying his inward anger, envy, and dejection, the outward sign of the sullen, wrathful heart, the fallen countenance marking the beginning of the path that leads to murder, God's question, v.6, addressing this very anger and dejection) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: unto Cain and to his offering he had not respect. And Cain was very wroth, and his countenance fell
the rejection of Cain's offering and his wrath
the rejection met with envy and rage instead of repentance
the fallen face betraying the sullen, wrathful heart

schools.summary: God questions Cain: 'and the LORD said unto Cain, Why art thou wroth? and why is thy countenance fallen?' — God, in mercy, questioning Cain about his anger and dejection (the question a gracious expostulation, meant to bring Cain to reflect on the unreasonableness of his wrath and to turn him from the path of sin, God reasoning with the angry man before he sins further); 'Why art thou wroth?' both a rebuke of Cain's groundless anger and a call to self-examination (Cain's wrath unjust — against God for a just rejection, and against his innocent brother — the question inviting Cain to consider and amend, God dealing patiently with the sinner); 'why is thy countenance fallen?' (God noting Cain's fallen countenance, the sign of his sullen, envious heart, the question pressing him to examine the cause of his dejection — not God's injustice but his own fault — the remedy, v.7, lying in doing well, God's gracious question seeking to forestall the coming sin) — Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: the LORD said unto Cain, Why art thou wroth? and why is thy countenance fallen?
God questioning Cain's anger
the gracious expostulation rebuking groundless wrath
God reasoning with the sinner to forestall the coming sin

schools.summary: The way of acceptance and the warning: 'if thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted (lifted up)? and if thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door' — God setting before Cain the way of acceptance (if he does well, with faith and a right heart, he will be accepted, his fallen countenance lifted up, the rejection not arbitrary, the remedy in Cain's own doing well). Keil and Delitzsch read 'sin (chattath) lieth (rovets — croucheth, as a beast of prey) at the door' — sin pictured as a wild beast crouching at the door, ready to spring on Cain if he does not well; and 'unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over him' of SIN — sin's desire toward Cain to master him, but he ought to rule over it (the same words as 3:16, applied to sin, the call to master the sin lying in wait).

Clarke: a second reading takes chattath as 'a sin-offering' — 'a sin-offering lieth (coucheth) at the door': even now a sin-offering is available to Cain at hand, if he will use it, the way of atonement open; and 'unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over him' Clarke refers not to sin but to ABEL — Abel's desire (deference) shall be to Cain as the elder, and Cain shall rule over him as the firstborn, so Cain need not envy, his birthright standing. The phrases turn on whether chattath is 'sin' (a crouching beast) or 'sin-offering' (at hand), and whether the desire/rule is sin's (to be mastered) or Abel's (the deferring younger brother).

debates.question: What does 'sin lieth at the door... unto thee shall be his desire' mean?

sides.position: 'Sin' crouches at the door like a beast; its desire is toward Cain to master him, but he must rule over it
'A sin-offering' lies at the door, available to Cain; and the desire/rule refers to Abel (the deferring younger brother, as the firstborn's right)

themes: If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted? and if thou doest not well, sin lieth at the door. And unto thee shall be his desire, and thou shalt rule over him
God's warning to Cain (the crux of 'sin lieth at the door')
sin as a crouching beast, or a sin-offering at hand
the desire and rule referring to sin (to be mastered) or to Abel

schools.summary: The first murder: 'and Cain talked with Abel his brother: and it came to pass, when they were in the field, that Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him' — Cain, harboring envy and wrath (vv.5-7), drawing his brother into the field (away from witnesses) and there killing him (the first murder, premeditated and treacherous, the wrath God warned against, v.7, issuing in fratricide); 'Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him' (the murder of a brother, the righteous by the wicked, 1 John 3:12, 'Cain... slew his brother. And wherefore? Because his own works were evil, and his brother's righteous,' the first death a murder and a fratricide, the fruit of envy, unbelief, and unmastered sin, the enmity of the serpent's seed against the woman's, 3:15, breaking out in blood); Cain not mastering the sin crouching at the door (v.7) but letting it master him (the unrepented wrath growing into murder, the dreadful example of sin unchecked, Abel, the first martyr, falling by his brother's hand) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Cain... when they were in the field, that Cain rose up against Abel his brother, and slew him
the first murder - Cain slaying Abel
the righteous slain by the wicked (1 John 3:12), the fruit of unmastered sin
the enmity of the serpent's seed breaking out in blood

schools.summary: God's question and Cain's denial: 'and the LORD said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother? And he said, I know not: Am I my brother's keeper?' — God questioning Cain, as he questioned Adam (3:9), not of ignorance but to bring Cain to confession and conviction (the question pressing the murderer to account for his brother, God giving Cain opportunity to confess); 'I know not' a brazen lie added to the murder (Cain denying knowledge of his brother's whereabouts, hardening himself in his sin, the murderer adding falsehood to bloodshed, the impenitence and hardness of Cain who will not confess); 'Am I my brother's keeper?' a rude, insolent rejection of responsibility (denying any duty toward his brother, the callous, defiant words of the hardened sinner, instead of repenting Cain disowning all concern for Abel, the cold-heartedness of the murderer who casts off both truth and brotherly duty, the contrast with God's care for the slain) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, Gill.

themes: the LORD said unto Cain, Where is Abel thy brother? And he said, I know not: Am I my brother's keeper?
God's question and Cain's hardened denial
the brazen lie and insolent denial of responsibility
the impenitence and hardness of the murderer

schools.summary: Abel's blood crying out: 'and he said, What hast thou done? the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground' — God now charging Cain directly, 'What hast thou done?' (an exclamation of horror and a charge of the dreadful deed, God, who knows all, confronting Cain with the enormity of his sin, the murder unable to be hidden from God); 'the voice of thy brother's blood (bloods, in the plural) crieth unto me from the ground' (Abel's shed blood crying out to God for vengeance, the blood, soaked into the ground, crying from the earth, the figure of the murdered innocent crying to God for justice, Hebrews 12:24, the blood of Abel; Revelation 6:10, the souls under the altar, God hearing the cry of the oppressed and avenging blood); the crying blood the witness against the murderer (though Cain denied and lied, v.9, the blood itself testifying, God the avenger of blood, the innocent blood crying out and God answering with judgment, vv.11-12, no murder hidden from God) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, JFB, Clarke.

themes: What hast thou done? the voice of thy brother's blood crieth unto me from the ground
Abel's blood crying out from the ground
the murdered innocent's blood crying to God for vengeance
the witness against Cain that no lie could silence

schools.summary: The curse on Cain: 'and now art thou cursed from the earth, which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand' — Cain cursed, the curse coming 'from the earth,' which had received Abel's blood (the very ground that drank the innocent blood becoming the instrument or sphere of Cain's curse, the murderer cursed in relation to the earth); whereas the ground was cursed for Adam's sin (3:17), now Cain himself cursed (a heavier curse on the person of the murderer, Cain cursed from/by the earth, which will no longer yield to him, v.12, the curse falling personally on Cain for his crime); 'which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand' (the ground, as it were, drinking the blood Cain shed, the blood from Cain's hand soaking into the earth, the figure marking both the enormity of the crime — the earth defiled with innocent blood — and the ground of the curse — the earth, defiled by Cain, turning against him) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: now art thou cursed from the earth, which hath opened her mouth to receive thy brother's blood from thy hand
the curse on Cain from the earth
the murderer personally cursed, a heavier curse than Adam's
the earth defiled by innocent blood turning against him

schools.summary: Cain's sentence: 'when thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength; a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth' — the curse striking Cain's calling (he was a tiller of the ground, v.2): the ground no longer yielding its strength (its full produce) to him (his labor frustrated, the earth barren to him, the murderer's toil cursed, his farming, already under the general curse, 3:17-19, now made fruitless, the earth not rewarding his toil, the murderer punished in the very calling he followed); 'a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth' (Cain condemned to a restless, wandering, unsettled life, driven from place to place, without rest or settled home, the figure of the guilty conscience and the outcast life of the murderer, Cain becoming a homeless wanderer, marked by his crime) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: When thou tillest the ground, it shall not henceforth yield unto thee her strength; a fugitive and a vagabond shalt thou be in the earth
Cain's sentence - the barren ground and the wandering life
the curse striking his calling, the earth fruitless to him
Cain condemned to a restless, homeless wandering

schools.summary: Cain's despairing complaint: 'and Cain said unto the LORD, My punishment is greater than I can bear' — the words rendered 'My punishment is greater than I can bear' (the common reading) or 'My iniquity is greater than can be forgiven' (an alternative rendering, the Hebrew avon meaning both 'iniquity' and 'punishment'); on the first reading, Cain complaining of the severity of his sentence (despair, not repentance, expressing not penitence but despairing complaint, feeling the weight of his punishment but not truly repenting, the sorrow of the world not godly sorrow, lamenting his misery not his sin, the despair of the hardened who feel the punishment but not the guilt); on the second reading, Cain despairing of forgiveness (his guilt too great to be forgiven); whether read as complaint of punishment or despair of pardon, Cain's words lacking true repentance (he not seeking mercy but bemoaning his lot, the contrast with the penitent who casts himself on God's mercy) — Henry, Calvin, Clarke, Gill.

themes: My punishment is greater than I can bear
Cain's despairing complaint (the rendering 'punishment' or 'iniquity... greater than can be forgiven')
the sorrow of the world, not godly sorrow
Cain bemoaning his misery without true repentance

schools.summary: Cain's lament and fear: 'behold, thou hast driven me out this day from the face of the earth; and from thy face shall I be hid; and I shall be a fugitive and a vagabond in the earth; and it shall come to pass, that every one that findeth me shall slay me' — Cain lamenting that he is driven out from his settled place and (more grievously) from God's presence and favor (the exile from the face of the earth, his land, and from God's face, his fellowship and the place of worship, the alienation of the murderer from God and home); Cain owning his sentence (v.12), a restless, wandering, homeless life (feeling the misery of the outcast lot, the punishment weighing on him); 'every one that findeth me shall slay me' (Cain fearing that, as a wandering outcast, he will be killed by any who find him — avengers of Abel's blood, or others — the guilty conscience dreading retribution, the murderer fearing the very violence he committed, the terror of the guilty, haunted by the fear of death) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: thou hast driven me out this day from the face of the earth; and from thy face shall I be hid... every one that findeth me shall slay me
Cain's lament over his fate and fear of being slain
the exile from his land and from God's presence
the terror of the guilty conscience dreading retribution

schools.summary: The sevenfold vengeance and the mark: 'and the LORD said unto him, Therefore whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold. And the LORD set a mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him' — God, in answer to Cain's fear (v.14), declaring that whoever kills Cain will be punished sevenfold (God protecting the life even of the murderer from private vengeance, the threat restraining any from killing Cain, the divine prerogative over life and vengeance asserted); God's sevenfold threat an act of restraint and, in a sense, mercy (Cain spared immediate death and protected from being slain, God reserving vengeance to himself, the murderer preserved alive to live out his cursed, wandering life as a warning, God's forbearance even toward the guilty); 'the LORD set a mark (a sign) upon Cain' (God setting a mark or sign for Cain's protection, whether a visible mark on his person or a sign given to him for assurance, the mark not primarily a brand of shame but a protective token, lest any finding him should kill him, the divine guarantee of Cain's preservation, God's mark shielding the murderer's life) — Gill, Ellicott, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: whosoever slayeth Cain, vengeance shall be taken on him sevenfold. And the LORD set a mark upon Cain, lest any finding him should kill him
the sevenfold vengeance threatened and the mark of Cain
God protecting the murderer's life, reserving vengeance to himself
the mark a protective token, sparing Cain to live out his cursed wandering

schools.summary: Cain departs to Nod: 'and Cain went out from the presence of the LORD, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden' — Cain departing from the place of God's special presence (the place of worship, perhaps near Eden, where God manifested himself, the murderer leaving the presence and fellowship of God, whether a willful departure or the consequence of his sentence, Cain becoming an outcast from God, the alienation of the sinner from God sealed); Cain's going out from God's presence marking his estrangement (he leaving the worship and fellowship of God, the wicked, unrepentant, withdrawing from God, Cain's departure the sad picture of the apostate, cut off from God's presence); 'dwelt in the land of Nod (which means 'wandering'), on the east of Eden' (Cain settling in the land of Nod, east of Eden, the name 'Nod' fitting his sentence as a fugitive and vagabond, further east from Eden, the direction of increasing distance from God's garden, Cain's dwelling-place marking his outcast, wandering estate) — Keil and Delitzsch, Henry, Calvin, JFB, Gill.

themes: Cain went out from the presence of the LORD, and dwelt in the land of Nod, on the east of Eden
Cain departing from God's presence to the land of Nod
the alienation of the unrepentant sinner from God
the land of 'wandering' fitting his fugitive sentence

schools.summary: Cain's son and city: 'and Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch: and he builded a city, and called the name of the city, after the name of his son, Enoch' — Cain having a son, Enoch (a different Enoch from the godly Enoch of Seth's line, 5:18), Cain's wife one of Adam's daughters (his sister or near kin, the race propagating from the first pair, the line of Cain beginning); 'he builded a city' (the first city, built by Cain, whether a fortified settlement, Cain, the restless wanderer, seeking security and a settled place against his fear, v.14, or the beginning of urban civilization in Cain's line, the city marking the development of human society but founded by the murderer and his line); 'called the name of the city, after the name of his son, Enoch' (naming the city after his son, whether to perpetuate his name and dynasty, or to establish a settled, secure life in defiance of his wandering sentence, Cain's line developing the arts of civilization, vv.20-22, yet apart from God, the city of Cain standing over against the people who call on the LORD, v.26) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: Cain knew his wife; and she conceived, and bare Enoch: and he builded a city, and called the name of the city, after the name of his son, Enoch
Cain's son Enoch and the building of the first city
the line of Cain beginning, the first city built
the development of civilization by the murderer's line apart from God

schools.summary: The genealogy of Cain's line: 'and unto Enoch was born Irad: and Irad begat Mehujael: and Mehujael begat Methusael: and Methusael begat Lamech' — the genealogy tracing Cain's line through five generations to Lamech (the descent from Cain through Enoch, Irad, Mehujael, Methusael, to Lamech, the line of the murderer's posterity recorded); the names marking the generations of Cain's line (the genealogy brief, listing only the succession to Lamech, in whom the line's character, vv.19-24, is displayed, the descendants of Cain multiplying and developing but apart from God); the line of Cain set over against the line of Seth (ch. 5), the two genealogies tracing the two seeds (3:15) — the line of Cain (the city-builders, the inventors, but godless) and the line of Seth (who call on the LORD, v.26), the genealogy of Cain leading to Lamech, the boastful avenger, vv.23-24, marking the line's moral decline — Gill, Ellicott.

themes: unto Enoch was born Irad: and Irad begat Mehujael: and Mehujael begat Methusael: and Methusael begat Lamech
the genealogy of Cain's line to Lamech
the line of the murderer's posterity, developing apart from God
the line of Cain set over against the line of Seth

schools.summary: Lamech's two wives: 'and Lamech took unto him two wives: the name of the one was Adah, and the name of the other Zillah' — Lamech, of Cain's line, taking two wives (the first recorded instance of polygamy, Lamech breaking the original ordinance of marriage — one man and one woman, one flesh, 2:24 — the corruption of marriage in Cain's godless line); Lamech's taking of two wives a departure from God's institution of marriage (2:24, where the two are one flesh, polygamy, contrary to the creation ordinance, beginning in Cain's line, the corruption of the marriage bond marking the moral decline of the ungodly seed, what God joined as one fallen man multiplying); the polygamy of Lamech, with his boastful violence (vv.23-24), marking the character of Cain's line (departing from God's order in marriage and in conduct, the ungodly line developing the arts, vv.20-22, but corrupting God's institutions, the contrast with the godly line of Seth) — Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Lamech took unto him two wives: the name of the one was Adah, and the name of the other Zillah
Lamech taking two wives - the first polygamy
the corruption of marriage, contrary to God's institution (2:24)
the moral decline of Cain's godless line

schools.summary: Jabal the herdsman: 'and Adah bare Jabal: he was the father of such as dwell in tents, and of such as have cattle' — Jabal, son of Lamech, the founder (father, originator) of the nomadic, pastoral way of life (dwelling in tents and keeping cattle, developing the art of herding and the tent-dwelling, migratory life, the first of the herdsmen-nomads); Jabal 'the father' the originator or pattern of the pastoral life (the arts of human society — herding here, music and metalwork, vv.21-22 — developing in Cain's line, the cultural advances of mankind arising even in the godless line, God's gifts of skill and invention spread though the line is apart from God); the arts and civilization advancing in Cain's line (Jabal the herdsman, Jubal the musician, Tubal-cain the metalworker, the development of human culture, yet this progress, in the line of Cain, apart from God and accompanied by moral decline — Lamech's polygamy and violence — the gifts of civilization and the absence of godliness coexisting in the seed of Cain) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: Adah bare Jabal: he was the father of such as dwell in tents, and of such as have cattle
Jabal, father of tent-dwelling herdsmen
the rise of the pastoral art in Cain's line
the arts of civilization developing apart from God

schools.summary: Jubal the musician: 'and his brother's name was Jubal: he was the father of all such as handle the harp and organ' — Jubal, brother of Jabal, the founder (father, originator) of music (the inventor or first master of the harp, a stringed instrument, and the organ, a wind/pipe instrument, developing the art of music, the first of the musicians); Jubal 'the father' the originator of instrumental music (the harp and organ, representing stringed and wind instruments, marking the development of music as an art, the cultural gift of music arising in Cain's line, the arts of civilization — herding, music, metalwork — developing together); music, like the pastoral and metalworking arts (vv.20, 22), a gift and skill developed in Cain's line (the arts advancing, yet apart from God, the use of music for God's glory or, in the godless line, for pleasure and worldliness, the development of culture in the seed of Cain, marked by both skill and ungodliness) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: his brother's name was Jubal: he was the father of all such as handle the harp and organ
Jubal, father of music
the rise of the musical art in Cain's line
the arts of civilization developing apart from God

schools.summary: Tubal-cain the metalworker: 'and Zillah, she also bare Tubalcain, an instructer of every artificer in brass and iron: and the sister of Tubalcain was Naamah' — Tubal-cain, son of Lamech, the founder of metalworking (the teacher and master of all who work in brass — copper/bronze — and iron, developing the art of forging metals, making tools, weapons, and implements, the first of the smiths); 'an instructer (sharpener, forger) of every artificer in brass and iron' the originator of metalworking (the working of metals marking a major advance in human civilization — tools, weapons, agriculture, war — the arts of metallurgy arising in Cain's line, the cultural gifts developing though apart from God); 'the sister of Tubalcain was Naamah' (Naamah, 'pleasant' or 'lovely,' named, perhaps as a notable woman of the line, the naming of a woman unusual in the genealogy, whether for her beauty, her notoriety, or some tradition, she recorded, the line of Cain, with its arts and its women, set down) — Gill, Clarke, JFB.

themes: Zillah... bare Tubalcain, an instructer of every artificer in brass and iron: and the sister of Tubalcain was Naamah
Tubal-cain, father of metalworking
the rise of metallurgy in Cain's line
the arts of civilization developed apart from God

schools.summary: Lamech's boastful song: 'and Lamech said unto his wives, Adah and Zillah, Hear my voice; ye wives of Lamech, hearken unto my speech: for I have slain a man to my wounding, and a young man to my hurt' — Lamech addressing his wives in a poetic, boastful speech (the first recorded poetry, a song of violence, summoning their attention to his vaunting words, the arrogant, self-glorying tone of the ungodly Lamech); 'I have slain a man to my wounding (for wounding me), and a young man to my hurt (for hurting me)' (Lamech boasting of having killed a man, and a young man, in revenge for a wound, whether he killed in self-defense or boasts of his readiness to kill any who cross him, the violence and vengefulness of Lamech, glorying in bloodshed, the moral decline of Cain's line now boasting of murder); Lamech's song revealing the spirit of Cain's line — violent, vengeful, and boastful (in contrast to Cain, who feared retribution, 4:14, Lamech glorying in his power to avenge himself many times over, v.24, the swaggering pride of the godless seed) — Gill, Henry, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: Lamech said unto his wives, Adah and Zillah, Hear my voice... for I have slain a man to my wounding, and a young man to my hurt
Lamech's boastful song of violence
the violent, vengeful spirit of Cain's line
the godless line glorying in bloodshed

schools.summary: Lamech's boast of vengeance: 'if Cain shall be avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech seventy and sevenfold' — Lamech, completing his boast (v.23), claiming that if Cain's death would be avenged sevenfold (4:15), his own would be avenged seventy-sevenfold (vaunting a far greater vengeance for himself, the swaggering self-assertion of the violent man); Lamech presumptuously applying God's word about Cain (the sevenfold vengeance, 4:15, which was God's protection of Cain) to his own case, magnifying it tenfold (boasting that he is more terrible than Cain, more to be avenged, the arrogant abuse of God's word, turning divine forbearance into a boast of self-vengeance); Lamech's 'seventy and sevenfold' of vengeance standing in striking contrast to Christ's 'seventy times seven' of forgiveness (Matthew 18:22, the line of Cain boasting of unlimited vengeance while the gospel calls for unlimited forgiveness, the spirit of Lamech and the spirit of Christ opposed, the moral character of Cain's line sealed in this boast) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: If Cain shall be avenged sevenfold, truly Lamech seventy and sevenfold
Lamech's boast of greater vengeance than Cain
the presumptuous abuse of God's word, boasting of unlimited self-vengeance
the contrast with Christ's unlimited forgiveness (Matthew 18:22)

schools.summary: The birth of Seth: 'and Adam knew his wife again; and she bare a son, and called his name Seth: For God, said she, hath appointed me another seed instead of Abel, whom Cain slew' — after the loss of Abel (slain) and the departure of Cain (cursed), Adam and Eve having another son, Seth (whose name relates to 'appointed' or 'set'), the birth of Seth beginning a new, godly line (ch. 5), through which the promised seed would come; Eve naming her son Seth, saying 'God hath appointed me another seed instead of Abel, whom Cain slew' (acknowledging God's gift of another seed to replace the murdered Abel, her words expressing faith and hope: God has not let the godly seed fail but has appointed another in Abel's place, the promise, 3:15, continuing through Seth, Eve's faith laying hold of God's provision); Seth replacing Abel as the head of the godly line (the seed of the woman, in contrast to the line of Cain, through Seth and his descendants, ch. 5, the line of promise continuing to Noah and ultimately to Christ, God preserving a godly seed despite the murder of Abel and the apostasy of Cain) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

themes: Adam knew his wife again; and she bare a son, and called his name Seth: For God, said she, hath appointed me another seed instead of Abel, whom Cain slew
the birth of Seth and the renewal of the godly line
God appointing another seed in Abel's place
the line of promise renewed in Seth

schools.summary: The beginning of calling on the LORD: 'and to Seth, to him also there was born a son; and he called his name Enos: then began men to call upon the name of the LORD' — Seth having a son, Enos (whose name means 'man,' especially man as frail, mortal), the godly line continuing through Seth to Enos. Keil and Calvin (and Gill) read 'then began men to call upon the name of the LORD' as the beginning of public worship and the invocation of God: men, in Seth's line, beginning to call upon (invoke, worship, pray to) the name of the LORD — the public, corporate worship of God beginning, the godly distinguishing themselves by their worship, the rise of religious assembly and prayer, in contrast to the godless line of Cain.

Alternative readings noted: a second (the margin) takes it as 'then began men to be called by the name of the LORD' — the godly beginning to be called the sons of God, distinguished by his name as his people; a third (the Jewish/Targumic) renders it negatively, 'then began profanation' / 'then men began to profane,' the beginning of idolatry, men beginning to profane the name or to make idols and worship the stars. The dominant Christian tradition (with Clarke) reads it of the beginning of public worship — the godly line of Seth beginning the open, public invocation and worship of the LORD, the rise of true religion in Enos's day.

debates.question: What does 'then began men to call upon the name of the LORD' mean?

sides.position: The beginning of public worship and the invocation of God — the godly line of Seth beginning corporate worship and prayer (the common Christian reading)
Began to be called by the name of the LORD — the godly distinguished as the sons of God, his people
Then profanation/idolatry began — men began to profane the name (the Jewish/Targumic reading)

themes: to Seth... there was born a son; and he called his name Enos: then began men to call upon the name of the LORD
the birth of Enos and the beginning of calling on the LORD
the crux of the phrase (public worship vs called by God's name vs profanation)
the godly line of Seth marked by the worship of God
